content: Large language models generate text through an autoregressive process, predicting each token sequentially based on preceding context. Initially, input text is tokenized and mapped into continuous embeddings. These embeddings feed into a transformer architecture, where self-attention mechanisms dynamically reweight token representations across the context window, capturing long-range dependencies and nuanced syntactic-semantic relationships. At each step, the model outputs a vector of logits representing unnormalized probabilities over the vocabulary; these logits are transformed via a softmax function into a probability distribution from which the next token is sampled or selected deterministically. Positional encodings are integral throughout, preserving token order within the otherwise permutation-invariant attention mechanism. Sampling strategies—such as temperature scaling or top-k/top-p filtering—modulate output diversity and coherence. The iterative nature of this process, combined with the model’s learned statistical patterns from vast corpora, enables generation of contextually relevant, fluent sequences. Model capacity and training data quality critically influence the fidelity and emergent capabilities observed in generated text.
